overview: WellNet works in the background and tries to keep your session inside a safer performance zone instead of forcing you to learn a control panel.
The mod watches connection instability and local frame instability at the same time, because either one can turn a good session into a rough one.
If the world is calm, WellNet stays quiet and avoids touching your settings.
If the game starts stuttering during world entry, host load, or short burst events, WellNet can temporarily hold a safer render distance.
That safe mode is not meant to be permanent. The mod also watches for recovery windows and slowly gives settings back when the session settles.
On a remote server, the mod mainly reacts to bursty traffic, ping spikes, and sudden frame stress that usually turn into visible rubber-banding or chunk hitching.
On a local hosted world, the mod is more careful because the same machine is drawing the world and running the integrated server.
The goal is not to chase pretty numbers. The goal is to stop one bad spike from turning into thirty seconds of visible chunk reloads or delayed gameplay.
When you ask for status, the normal status page is written for a regular player, not for a network engineer.
If you want the raw details, use /wellnet status technical and you will still get the metrics, reasons, and recent decision trace.
If you play giant modpacks, the most valuable part of WellNet is often the burst control and the slow recovery ladder after the burst is gone.
If you host a local world for friends, the most valuable part is usually the host-aware profile that protects the session when your machine is carrying both roles.
If you see WellNet do nothing, that usually means it decided your session does not currently need protection.
If you see WellNet protect the world, it should also tell you why in plain words instead of only showing cryptic scores.
Use the guide topics below when you want a quick explanation without opening config files.
Use /wellnet help status if you want a breakdown of what the player-friendly status page actually means.
Use /wellnet help hosting if you mostly play or host local integrated worlds.
Use /wellnet help traffic if you mostly care about bursty network play and server-side hitching.
WellNet is strongest when it reacts early to a bad direction, not after the session has already completely fallen apart.
Think of it as a stability buffer: small, quiet corrections before lag spikes become a full visible mess.
status: The first line of the status page tells you the overall mood of the session: stable, watching, protecting, recovering, or still analyzing.
Stable means the mod is seeing enough evidence that the session is currently healthy.
Watching means nothing critical is happening yet, but the mod has noticed enough roughness to stay alert.
Protecting means WellNet has moved into a safer mode because it believes a visible rough patch is either happening now or very close.
Recovering means WellNet thinks the dangerous part is over and is slowly restoring settings instead of snapping them back all at once.
The summary line tells you whether the decision is based on actual network statistics or on frame and traffic behavior when ping data is not trustworthy.
If the status talks about frame-analysis mode, it usually means you are in a local host scenario or the current session has no reliable ping window yet.
The action line tells you whether WellNet is holding steady, protecting, or recovering.
The reason line tells you what kind of pressure triggered that behavior: host load, burst spikes, frame spikes, unstable traffic, or world-entry settling.
The tip line is intentionally practical. It is the one line you should read if you want to know what the mod thinks is happening in normal language.
The footer reminds you that a technical page still exists when you want to inspect the raw metrics and trace history.
If the status stays in protecting mode for a long time, check whether the world is still generating chunks heavily or whether the host machine is actually under real load.
If the status keeps bouncing between protecting and recovering, that usually points to a session that is hovering around the safe threshold.
If the status says it is analyzing, that usually means there is not enough reliable data yet and it is intentionally waiting before touching anything.
A good status page should reduce confusion. It should tell you what is happening, not make you decode abbreviations by hand.
That is why the normal status now avoids leading with raw q-scores and instead starts with plain language.
The technical page is still there because tuning and debugging still matter during development and testing.
If the action says safe mode but your world still feels fine, wait a little first: WellNet prefers to recover gradually rather than overreact twice in a row.
If the action says stable and the world still looks rough, it usually means another mod or the server is the actual bottleneck rather than WellNet itself.
Treat the status as a session explanation tool, not as a magic quality grade.
hosting: Local hosting is a special case because one machine is doing two jobs at once: rendering your client and simulating the server for everyone connected.
That means a spike in frame time can quickly become a spike in server responsiveness, and then both sides start feeling bad together.
WellNet uses a host-aware profile so it can react earlier in local worlds than it would on a remote server.
The host-aware profile is not supposed to punish a healthy local world. It is supposed to stop a heavy chunk-load or burst moment from snowballing.
If WellNet enters host protection, it usually means it saw real pressure in frame spikes, burst behavior, or a combination of both.
During world start or heavy exploration, local hosting often experiences short violent spikes that look worse than the steady state a minute later.
Because of that, WellNet separates startup settling from long-term host pressure as much as possible.
When the world calms down, recovery should happen gradually and predictably instead of bouncing all the way back in one risky jump.
If you are hosting for friends, stable pacing matters more than chasing the highest possible render distance in every moment.
A slightly smaller render distance for one minute is usually much better than a visible freeze storm that affects everyone in the session.
If your local world never recovers, the likely cause is real sustained load from world generation, entity count, automation, or another heavy mod.
In those cases WellNet is acting like a pressure valve, not the source of the problem.
The host guide is mainly useful if you run big kitchensink packs, heavy exploration, or invite other players into an integrated server world.
If you only play solo in a light pack, the host profile may barely activate at all, which is a good sign rather than a bug.
WellNet should make a local hosted world feel less fragile during bad moments and less confusing when protection is active.
If the world is smooth, the best outcome is that you forget the mod is even there.
If the world starts hitching badly, the best outcome is that the mod explains the reason and contains the damage early.
Local hosting is where recovery logic matters most, because the wrong oscillation pattern can reload chunks and make the problem look worse than it is.
That is why the host profile favors strict burst handling but slower, more deliberate recovery.
Use the technical status page if you want to compare what the player-friendly message says against the raw frame trace.
traffic: Traffic pressure is not only about average bandwidth. Bad sessions often feel rough because the flow arrives in uneven bursts rather than a smooth rhythm.
WellNet watches burstiness, short spike windows, and sudden changes in session quality so it can react before that unevenness becomes obvious lag.
A remote session can have decent average ping and still feel terrible if burstiness and spike clustering are high.
That is why the mod does not trust a single average number when deciding whether to protect the client.
The traffic profile is most useful on servers that are technically playable but feel inconsistent, especially during teleports, chunk delivery, or heavy packet events.
If WellNet detects a real burst pattern, it can enter a short protective freeze window to avoid rapid-fire decision changes.
That freeze window is there to prevent the mod from changing course three times during one unstable burst.
When the burst window passes, the recovery ladder takes over and returns settings more slowly than the burst arrived.
This is intentional. Fast degradation and slower recovery usually feels better than equally fast jumps in both directions.
WellNet also keeps a memory of what a normal session for the current server looks like, so it can compare a rough patch against a more realistic baseline.
That baseline logic matters because a modded server can have a very different normal rhythm than vanilla.
What feels normal on one pack or one server can be a warning sign on another.
If you mainly care about network play, the two biggest wins are usually burst detection and the recovery ladder after the burst is gone.
If your network is calm, WellNet should do very little.
If your network goes rough for a short moment, WellNet should avoid turning that moment into a long visible mess.
If you see a traffic-related status tip, it means the mod believes the session rhythm is rough, not necessarily that your internet is bad in a simple average-ping sense.
Traffic protection is most valuable when the world is playable but choppy. That is exactly the zone where average ping alone is misleading.
The mod cannot fix the whole route to a server, but it can stop your client settings from adding more pressure during unstable windows.
That is what the traffic profile is trying to optimize for: smoother feel, fewer chain spikes, and faster stabilization after a burst.
Use the technical page if you want to inspect the raw burst and trace context behind a traffic-related decision.
multiplayer: On multiplayer servers, the goal is to make unstable moments feel shorter and less destructive, not to constantly rewrite settings for no reason.
A good multiplayer safeguard reacts to bad bursts, then gets out of the way when the session becomes healthy again.
WellNet therefore separates the first join phase, unstable burst moments, and the later steady phase.
Join time is special because the world is often loading chunks, entities, and UI state all at once.
If the mod acted as if that moment were the long-term baseline, it would learn the wrong lesson and keep the session pinned unnecessarily.
That is why the traffic phase profiler treats the first phase differently from steady-state multiplayer.
When a server calms down, the recovery ladder starts moving settings back instead of staying in protection forever.
When a server becomes rough again, the dampener can temporarily hold decisions to avoid jittering between two modes.
This is especially important on busy modded servers where one short packet storm can be followed by thirty seconds of normal play.
If your server uses custom plugins, fake player packets, or heavy scripted events, the technical status page will usually be more informative than average ping alone.
The normal status page is still useful because it answers the simple question first: is WellNet calm, protecting, or recovering right now?
You do not need to understand every network metric to benefit from the mod in multiplayer.
What matters is that the mod can explain why it changed something and why it is still holding or releasing that change.
If the page says the session is stable, that means WellNet currently believes protection would not give you a real benefit.
If the page says the session is protecting, that means it saw enough evidence to believe immediate stability matters more than aggressive settings.
If the page says recovering, the most important part is patience: the mod is intentionally waiting for consistency instead of reacting to one lucky second.
That slower recovery is what keeps bursty sessions from oscillating too hard.
Multiplayer protection is therefore mostly about timing and confidence, not just about raw ping values.
The better those two are, the less noticeable the mod becomes during healthy play and the more useful it becomes during rough play.
That is the behavior WellNet is trying to reach.
recovery: Recovery is the part of the system that gives settings back after the session improves.
In practice this is often more important than the downshift itself, because bad recovery logic creates visible oscillation.
If recovery is too fast, the same burst comes back and the client is forced into safe mode again immediately.
If recovery is too slow, the player feels stuck and assumes the mod is simply holding the world hostage.
WellNet therefore uses a recovery ladder rather than one giant jump.
The ladder looks for repeated signs of stability instead of trusting one lucky quiet second.
On a local hosted world, recovery is intentionally slower because the cost of one wrong jump is higher.
On a remote server, recovery can usually move sooner once the burst window is clearly gone.
The new player-friendly status is designed to make that difference understandable without making you read raw internal counters.
If the action says recovering, that does not mean the world is perfect again. It means the mod believes the session is consistently healthier than the danger window.
Recovery should feel boring in the best way possible: one small step at a time, no dramatic flicker, no hidden cliff in the middle.
A recovery ladder is also useful because modded sessions often improve in layers rather than all at once.
Chunk generation might calm down first, then frame spikes improve, then the packet rhythm settles after that.
A ladder handles that shape better than a single hard on/off threshold.
If the world still feels rough during recovery, the mod may pause the climb or step back into protection if the evidence turns bad again.
That pause is a feature, not a failure. It is what keeps the system from lying to itself about recovery.
When recovery is working properly, the player mostly notices the absence of repeated chunk flicker and big render-distance swings.
When recovery is broken, the player notices the mod immediately and assumes the mod is the problem.
That is why this part of the logic deserves as much attention as the initial spike detection.
A boring recovery curve is usually a very good sign.
troubleshooting: If WellNet says the session is stable but the game still feels awful, the bottleneck is probably somewhere outside the mod's control.
That could be another client mod, heavy server-side scripting, entity overload, disk stalls, or shader or render problems.
If WellNet says it is protecting and the world immediately feels better, the safeguard is doing useful work.
If WellNet says it is protecting and nothing improves at all, the world may be under real sustained load rather than a short burst problem.
Use the technical status page when you want to compare the plain-language message against raw metrics and recent trace history.
If traffic reads as zero forever in a real multiplayer session, the traffic hook probably needs more validation on that loader or environment.
If the status stays in analyzing forever, it usually means the session never produced a reliable enough data window.
If the status tip keeps mentioning host load in a local world, check whether chunk generation or automation is still running heavily.
If the status tip keeps mentioning bursts on multiplayer, compare the rough moments against teleports, chunk loads, plugin actions, or packet-heavy gameplay.
Remember that the mod is trying to reduce the damage of instability, not to hide every possible problem from every layer of the stack.
A good troubleshooting habit is to look at what the mod claims, then compare it against what you were actually doing in the game at that second.
Was the world loading, were you flying fast, did you teleport, did someone join, did the server trigger a scripted event, or did a machine-heavy base finish loading?
Those gameplay events often explain burst patterns better than staring at one metric line alone.
If you need to report a WellNet bug, a fresh latest.log plus one technical status snapshot is much more useful than a vague it lagged report.
If you are testing a change to the mod itself, compare the same scenario before and after instead of comparing two unrelated worlds.
That gives you a real signal instead of noise.
Troubleshooting is easier when the status page speaks human language first and raw metrics second.
That is exactly why the player-facing report exists.
If you are lost, start with /wellnet status, then move to /wellnet status technical only if the simple page is not enough.
That two-step flow is intentional.
faq: Does WellNet always lower render distance? No. The best case is usually that the mod sees no reason to interfere and leaves your settings alone.
Why does WellNet sometimes react more strongly in a local world than on a remote server? Because local hosting means one machine is carrying both client rendering and server simulation.
Why is recovery slower than protection? Because fast recovery feels good for one second and then usually creates a second burst if the session was not truly stable yet.
Does a stable status mean the server is perfect? No. It only means WellNet currently believes extra client-side protection would not materially help.
Does a protecting status always mean the internet is bad? No. It can also mean frame spikes, chunk loading, host pressure, or another burst source.
Why does the guide talk about rhythm instead of only ping? Because many rough sessions feel bad due to uneven burst timing even when the average ping number is not dramatic.
Why keep a technical status page if the mod is aimed at normal players? Because real debugging still needs trace history and raw metrics.
Why does WellNet use phases? Because joining, steady play, local hosting, and burst recovery should not all be judged by the same expectations.
Can WellNet help on a heavy modpack even if the PC is not very strong? Yes, that is one of the main places where early burst control and gradual recovery actually matter.
If I never see WellNet activate, is that bad? Usually no. It often means your session is already healthy enough that extra protection is unnecessary.
If the status says analyzing, should I worry? Not usually. That state simply means the mod is waiting for enough trustworthy data before making claims.
Why does WellNet prefer one calm step up instead of a large snap back? Because large snaps are more likely to restart the exact stutter pattern it just contained.

Can the mod know whether the problem is definitely the server or definitely the client? Not with perfect certainty, which is why it uses confidence and phase-aware heuristics instead of one absolute claim.
Why is there a guide inside the jar at all? Because the command system becomes much more useful when the explanation travels with the mod and stays available in-game.
What should I check first if the world still feels bad? Look at the plain status page, then compare the reason to what was happening in the game at the same moment.
Why does WellNet talk about safer settings instead of optimal settings? Because during unstable windows, the safest stable choice is often better than the most aggressive one.
What makes the mod stronger after this update? Clearer phases, better burst containment, friendlier status output, and more internal trace context.
Why is there more code now? Because the new behavior is split into dedicated subsystems instead of hiding everything inside one big heuristic blob.
Why is the jar larger now? Because it now includes more real behavior, a localized guide layer, policy tracing, and player-facing status resources.
What is the intended feel of the mod? Quiet when the session is healthy, visible only when the session actually needs help.
glossary: Burst: a short window where multiple bad spikes arrive close together and the session feels much worse than the average number suggests.
Settling: the phase right after joining a world or after a heavy state change where the mod expects temporary noise and avoids trusting it too quickly.
Steady phase: the calmer part of the session where the mod expects the current world or server to behave closer to its normal rhythm.
Host pressure: local strain caused by the same machine running the client and the integrated server at the same time.
Recovery ladder: a gradual sequence of upward steps that restores settings more carefully than the initial protection step down.
Safe mode: the temporary state where WellNet chooses stability over aggressive client settings because the current session looks rough enough to justify it.
Frame-analysis mode: a fallback path used when ping statistics are missing or not trustworthy enough, so the mod leans more on frame and traffic behavior.
Burstiness: how uneven the traffic flow is compared with its average level, which often matters more than the average number by itself.
Trace: a short internal history of recent policy decisions that helps explain why the mod changed or held a setting.
Confidence: how strongly the mod believes the current signal is actionable rather than random noise.
Protecting: the player-facing word used when the mod has chosen to actively defend smoothness.
Recovering: the player-facing word used when the mod believes the dangerous window has passed and settings can climb back carefully.
Analyzing: the state where the mod is still collecting enough evidence to avoid lying about a session too early.
Watching: the state between calm and active protection, where the mod sees roughness but not yet a strong enough reason to clamp hard.
Player-facing status: the simplified report written for normal players instead of for people reading raw metrics all day.
diagnostics: The technical status page exists so you can inspect raw values without losing the simple page that normal players need.
Use /wellnet status technical when you want to see metrics, the current action plan, and the recent policy trace all in one place.
The trace is especially helpful because it shows whether the mod actually changed something or whether it merely kept observing.
If the same reason appears across several trace entries, the mod is seeing consistent evidence rather than one random spike.
If the trace alternates rapidly, that usually means the session is hovering close to a threshold and deserves a closer look.
When the plain status page says protecting, the technical page helps you see whether that came from burst guard, host pressure, traffic pressure, or raw frame stress.
When the plain status page says recovering, the technical page shows how fresh that decision is and what live score the mod sees now.
That age marker matters because an old decision note should not be confused with the current live session quality.
Diagnostics are also useful for comparing one build of the mod against another in the same world or server scenario.
If you are validating a change, repeat the same kind of join, movement, chunk load, or host situation and compare the trace before and after.
The goal of diagnostics is not to overwhelm the player with numbers. The goal is to make the mod explainable when you do need the numbers.
A strong status system should let a normal player stop at the simple page and let an advanced user drill into the technical page only when necessary.
That layered approach is why the command system now exposes both friendly and technical views.
If a guide topic is too abstract, use diagnostics together with a fresh latest.log so you can compare what the command said with what the session recorded.
Good diagnostics shorten the distance between a symptom, a probable cause, and a real fix.
trace: The trace page is the short story of WellNet's recent decisions.
Use /wellnet trace when you want to know what the mod believed a few seconds ago instead of only what it believes right now.
That matters because one rough burst can trigger protection, and then the session may already be recovering by the time you open the status page.
A recent trace helps you tell the difference between a current problem and a problem that already passed.
Each entry includes the recent session phase, the main reason, and whether WellNet actually changed render distance or only kept watching.
This is useful when you are trying to understand whether the mod is nervous or whether it is following a stable pattern.
If the last few entries all mention the same reason, that usually means the evidence stayed consistent.
If the entries change direction every few seconds, that usually means the session is hovering near a threshold or another mod is creating alternating pressure.
Trace history is not only for debugging. It is also useful for normal play because it answers a simple question: what just happened and why did the mod react?
The trace page is intentionally written in plain language rather than in compact internal abbreviations.
If you need the raw numbers behind a trace entry, pair it with /wellnet status technical.
If you only need the story of the session, /wellnet trace is usually enough.
phases: WellNet does not judge every second of play by the same rules. It splits the session into phases first.
Join is the first contact with a world or server, where chunk delivery, player data, UI, and other systems often all wake up at once.
Settling is the short period after that, where the world may still be noisy but the mod is starting to trust the data more.
Steady is the calmer long-running phase where the mod expects the server or world to behave closer to its normal rhythm.
Host local is a special phase for integrated-server worlds where one machine is both the player client and the host.
Burst is the phase WellNet uses after a short violent instability window, when it wants to prevent repeated back-and-forth decisions.
These phases are important because a harsh join spike should not be treated as the permanent baseline for the rest of the session.
Without phases, a mod often learns the wrong lesson and either clamps too early or recovers too late.
The phase profiler is therefore one of the biggest code-structure improvements in this version.
It turns one big heuristic blob into a clearer session model with more predictable behavior.
The player-friendly status page now mentions the current phase so normal players can understand whether the world is still settling or already in steady play.
If the phase is wrong, the rest of the policy often becomes wrong too. That is why phase detection deserves its own subsystem.
servers: Different servers do not have the same normal rhythm, especially in modded play.
A stable vanilla-like world and a heavy scripted kitchen-sink pack can both be healthy while producing very different traffic patterns.
WellNet now keeps a short session profile for the current server or world so it can compare a rough moment against a more realistic baseline.
That profile is not trying to memorize everything forever. It is there to avoid treating every server as if it should look identical.
If a server is always a little spiky but still playable, the profile helps WellNet avoid panicking too early.
If a server suddenly becomes much rougher than its own recent baseline, the profile helps WellNet react faster because the change stands out more clearly.
This matters on big modpacks where normal traffic shape is already different from lightweight play.
It also matters for local hosting, because a host session can have its own repeatable pattern that differs from remote play.
The server profile works together with the phase profiler: one says what part of the session you are in, the other says what normal looks like for that session.
Together they make the mod more strict when it should be strict and less noisy when it should stay calm.
This is a structural change, not only a threshold tweak.
It makes future tuning easier because the code now has a real place for baseline memory instead of hiding it inside one overloaded decision method.
lagspikes: Lag spikes are rarely a single number problem. They usually arrive as a short chain of bad moments that stack together.
That is why WellNet now uses a spike dampener instead of trusting only one quality score at one moment in time.
The dampener looks at short bad windows, how quickly they repeat, and whether the session is actually calming down afterward.
When the dampener sees a real burst event, it can temporarily freeze decision changes so the mod does not thrash between two modes.
That freeze window is important because visual flicker often comes from repeated toggles rather than from one single protective step.
The recovery ladder then handles the way back up, which is intentionally slower and more careful than the drop into protection.
This combination is one of the main anti-lagspike features in the new code.
It reduces the chance that one ugly burst turns into repeated chunk reloads, repeated render-distance swings, or a long staircase of tiny interventions.
The dampener also makes trace history easier to read because the reasons become more coherent across a burst sequence.
If the world feels rough in short violent pulses, this is the subsystem that is most likely helping you.
If the world is healthy, the dampener should be almost invisible.
That is the correct outcome: strong containment when needed, quiet behavior when not needed.

tips.default: WellNet is trying to keep the session smooth without making you babysit settings every minute.
tips.analyzing: The mod is still reading the session. Wait a short moment before judging the world state.
tips.protecting: A temporary safer profile is active because the session looked rough enough to justify it.
tips.recovering: The rough patch looks weaker now, so WellNet is giving settings back gradually instead of all at once.
tips.host_pressure: Your machine is carrying both client rendering and local hosting, so WellNet is protecting the world from chain spikes.
tips.burst_guard: A short burst was strong enough to trigger a protective cooldown so the mod does not bounce between decisions.
tips.frame_spikes: The mod is reacting to frame instability, which usually feels like chunk hitching or sudden freezes rather than pure network lag.
tips.traffic_pressure: The packet flow looks uneven enough that WellNet is preferring stability over aggressive client settings.
tips.join_settling: The world is still settling after entry, so WellNet is staying conservative until the session looks more trustworthy.
tips.hosting: Local hosted worlds benefit most when protection is calm and predictable rather than aggressive.
tips.stable_network: The connection currently looks healthy, so the best outcome is that WellNet stays quiet and out of the way.
tips.watching_network: The session is playable, but the mod sees enough roughness to keep watching for a real burst.
tips.stable_frames: Frame-based fallback says the world is mostly settled, so the next steps should be recovery rather than more downshifts.
